title: Forces and momentum
summary: Forces change motion, and momentum measures the motion that forces change. This lesson builds the whole mechanics toolkit of A.2: Newton's three laws, free-body (force) diagrams, the common types of force (weight, normal, tension, friction, drag, buoyancy and the spring force from Hooke's law), translational equilibrium, Newton's second law in both its forms ($F = ma$ and $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$), momentum and impulse, conservation of linear momentum, elastic versus inelastic collisions and explosions, and uniform circular motion with its centripetal force. It is a calculation-heavy Paper 2 topic, so it is built around fully worked examples marked exactly the way our engine marks your own practice, with method (M) marks, answer (A) marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) shown line by line.
learningObjectives: State and apply Newton's three laws of motion, and draw correct free-body (force) diagrams for objects in equilibrium and accelerating.
Identify and calculate the common contact and field forces: weight, normal, tension, static and kinetic friction, drag, buoyancy and the spring force ($F = -kx$).
Apply Newton's second law in both forms, $F = ma$ and $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$, and use impulse $J = F\Delta t = \Delta p$.
Apply conservation of linear momentum to collisions and explosions, and distinguish elastic from inelastic collisions using kinetic energy.
Analyse uniform circular motion using centripetal acceleration $a = v^2/r$ and centripetal force $F = mv^2/r$.
Present working so that method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL; some circular-motion/momentum extensions are HL.

simpleExplanation.title: Why things speed up, slow down and turn
simpleExplanation.summary: A force is a push or a pull. On its own an object keeps doing whatever it was doing; only an unbalanced force changes its motion. Momentum, $p = mv$, is the 'quantity of motion' an object carries, and Newton's second law says a force is really the rate at which momentum changes.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a shopping trolley. Empty and still, it stays put until you push it (first law). Push harder, or load it lighter, and it speeds up more for the same push (second law). Shove it into a wall and the wall shoves back just as hard on the trolley (third law) — which is why your hands sting. And to steer it round a corner you have to keep pulling it inwards the whole time; let go and it runs straight on (circular motion).
steps: Draw a free-body diagram: one dot for the object, one labelled arrow for every force acting ON it, and nothing else.
Decide whether the forces are balanced. Balanced means equilibrium — constant velocity or rest. Unbalanced means acceleration.
Apply $\Sigma F = ma$ along each direction, or use momentum $p = mv$ and impulse $J = F\Delta t = \Delta p$ when forces act over a time.
For collisions and explosions use conservation of momentum; for circular motion set the net inward force equal to $mv^2/r$.

flashcards.back: An object continues at rest or at constant velocity unless acted on by a resultant (net) external force. This defines inertia and tells you that constant velocity means the forces are balanced.

The resultant force on an object equals its rate of change of momentum: $F = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$. For constant mass this reduces to $F = ma$. Force and acceleration are vectors in the same direction.

If body A exerts a force on body B, then B exerts an equal and opposite force on A. The two forces are the SAME TYPE, equal in size, opposite in direction, and — crucially — act on DIFFERENT bodies, so they never cancel.
A diagram showing a single object as a point (or box) with one arrow for every force acting ON it, each labelled. Forces the object exerts on other things are NOT drawn.

The gravitational force on a mass: $W = mg$, directed vertically downward, where $g$ is the gravitational field strength (9.81 N kg⁻¹ near Earth's surface). Weight is a force in newtons; mass in kilograms is not.

The contact force a surface exerts perpendicular to itself. On a horizontal surface with no vertical acceleration $N = mg$, but on an incline or in a lift $N \ne mg$ — always find it from the free-body diagram.

Static friction adjusts up to a maximum to prevent sliding; kinetic friction acts on a sliding object and is roughly constant. Both oppose relative motion (or its tendency) and act along the surface. Maximum $f = \mu N$.

Tension is the pull along a string or rope (same magnitude throughout an ideal light string). Drag is a resistive force from a fluid, opposite to motion and increasing with speed. Buoyancy (upthrust) is the upward force a fluid exerts on a submerged object.

For an ideal spring the restoring force is proportional to the extension: $F = -kx$, where $k$ is the spring constant (N m⁻¹) and $x$ the extension or compression. The minus sign shows the force opposes the displacement.

A body is in translational equilibrium when the resultant force on it is zero ($\Sigma F = 0$). It is then either at rest or moving at constant velocity — acceleration is zero. Resolve forces into components and set each direction's sum to zero.
$p = mv$, a vector (kg m s⁻¹) pointing along the velocity. The resultant force equals the rate of change of momentum, which is why momentum is central to collisions and impacts.
$J = F\Delta t = \Delta p$. The impulse of a force (its size times the time it acts) equals the change in momentum it produces. It is the area under a force–time graph. Units: N s = kg m s⁻¹.

When no external resultant force acts, the total momentum of a system is constant: $\Sigma p_{\text{before}} = \Sigma p_{\text{after}}$. Holds in every collision and explosion; momentum is a vector, so assign signs to directions.

Momentum is conserved in BOTH. Kinetic energy is conserved only in a perfectly elastic collision. In an inelastic collision (especially when objects stick together) some kinetic energy becomes heat, sound or deformation.

The NET inward force keeping an object on a circular path: $F = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} = m\omega^2 r$, directed toward the centre. It is not a new kind of force — it is provided by tension, friction, gravity or normal force.

There is no real outward force on an object in circular motion. The feeling of being flung outward is inertia — the body's tendency to go straight while the centripetal force pulls it inward. Never add an outward force to a free-body diagram.

quickCheck.prompt: A book rests on a table. The Newton's-third-law partner to the Earth's gravitational pull on the book (its weight) is:
options: The normal force of the table pushing up on the book
The gravitational pull of the book on the Earth
The weight of the table
The book pressing down on the table
quickCheck.answer: The gravitational pull of the book on the Earth

quickCheck.prompt: Two railway trucks collide and couple together, moving off as one. Which statement is correct?
options: Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved
Kinetic energy is conserved but momentum is not
Momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not
Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved
quickCheck.answer: Momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not

quickCheck.prompt: A car travels at constant speed around a circular bend. The centripetal force on the car is best described as:
options: An extra outward (centrifugal) force acting on the car
An additional force that must be added to the weight and normal force
The net inward force, here supplied by friction between the tyres and the road
Zero, because the car's speed is not changing
quickCheck.answer: The net inward force, here supplied by friction between the tyres and the road

quickCheck.prompt: A crate is pushed to the right and slides across a rough floor at constant velocity. The kinetic friction force on the crate acts:
options: To the right, in the direction of motion
To the left, opposing the motion
Upward, balancing the crate's weight
Nowhere — there is no friction at constant velocity
quickCheck.answer: To the left, opposing the motion

quickCheck.prompt: A ball undergoes a momentum change of 4.0 kg m s⁻¹ while a bat is in contact with it for 0.020 s. The average force on the ball is:

faq.q: If Newton's third law says forces are always equal and opposite, why does anything ever accelerate?
faq.a: Because the two forces in a third-law pair act on DIFFERENT objects, so they can never cancel each other. When you push a trolley, the trolley pushes back on you with an equal force — but the push on the trolley acts on the trolley, and the reaction acts on you. To find whether the trolley accelerates you look only at the forces acting on the trolley (your push, friction, etc.), and those need not balance. Confusing a third-law pair (two bodies) with balanced forces on one body (equilibrium) is one of the most common errors in the whole topic.

faq.q: When is momentum conserved but kinetic energy not?
faq.a: Momentum is conserved in every collision or explosion where no external resultant force acts — always, whether the collision is elastic or inelastic. Kinetic energy is only conserved in a perfectly elastic collision. In an inelastic collision, especially one where the objects stick together, some kinetic energy is transferred to heat, sound and permanent deformation, so the kinetic energy after is less than before even though the momentum is unchanged. So 'momentum conserved, KE not' is the signature of an inelastic collision.

faq.q: What exactly provides the centripetal force?
faq.a: Centripetal force is not a new force you add on — it is the name for the NET inward force that the ordinary forces already supply. For a ball on a string it is the tension; for a car on a flat bend it is friction between tyres and road; for a satellite it is gravity; for a bucket swung in a vertical circle at the top it is weight plus (or minus) the normal force. You identify the real forces, resolve toward the centre, and set their resultant equal to $mv^2/r$.

faq.q: Which form of Newton's second law should I use, $F = ma$ or $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$?
faq.a: They are the same law. Use $F = ma$ when the mass is constant and you want an acceleration — the standard free-body problem. Use $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$ (equivalently impulse $J = F\Delta t = \Delta p$) when a force acts over a stated time, when you are given a change in momentum, or in impact problems such as a ball bouncing off a wall or a rocket. The impulse form is also what you read off the area under a force–time graph.

sections.content: Almost every mechanical question in physics — how fast something accelerates, how hard a collision hits, why a satellite stays in orbit — comes back to two ideas: forces change motion, and momentum measures the motion that forces change. This lesson is the mechanics engine of the course. We move deliberately through Newton's three laws, the forces you meet in real problems, equilibrium, momentum and impulse, collisions and circular motion, and every worked example is laid out exactly the way marks are awarded, because in a Paper 2 calculation HOW you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
Newton's three laws of motion

Newton's laws are the foundation of the whole topic. The first law states that an object stays at rest or moves at constant velocity unless a resultant external force acts on it; this is the principle of inertia, and it tells you that constant velocity (including rest) always means the forces are balanced. The second law states that the resultant force equals the rate of change of momentum, $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$, which for constant mass becomes $F = ma$; force and acceleration are vectors pointing the same way. The third law states that if body A pushes on body B, then B pushes back on A with an equal and opposite force — the two forces are the same type, equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and act on different bodies.

items: First law (inertia): no resultant force ⟺ zero acceleration ⟺ rest or constant velocity.
Second law: $\Sigma F = ma$ for constant mass; more generally $\Sigma F = \Delta p / \Delta t$.
Third law: forces come in equal-and-opposite pairs of the SAME type, acting on TWO DIFFERENT bodies — so a third-law pair never cancels.
A third-law pair (two bodies) is not the same as balanced forces on one body (equilibrium).

sections.content: Free-body diagrams and the types of force

A free-body diagram represents a single chosen object as a dot or box, with one labelled arrow for every force acting ON it — and nothing else. Getting this diagram right is the most reliable way to score method marks, because every later equation reads off it. The forces you meet are of two kinds. Field forces act at a distance: weight $W = mg$ (always vertically down). Contact forces act where surfaces or media touch: the normal force $N$ (perpendicular to a surface), tension $T$ (a pull along a string, the same magnitude throughout an ideal light string), friction (along the surface, opposing relative motion or its tendency), drag (a resistive fluid force opposite to motion that grows with speed), buoyancy or upthrust (the upward force of a fluid on a submerged body), and the spring force.

Friction comes in two regimes. Static friction acts on a stationary object and adjusts itself, up to a maximum, to prevent sliding; kinetic friction acts on a sliding object and is roughly constant. The maximum friction on a surface is $f = \mu N$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $N$ the normal force — so friction depends on how hard the surfaces are pressed together, not on the contact area. For a spring, Hooke's law gives a restoring force proportional to the extension.
F = -kx

where $F$ is the spring force, $k$ is the spring constant (N m⁻¹) and $x$ is the extension or compression from the natural length. The minus sign records that the force always points back toward the equilibrium position, opposing the displacement.
Translational equilibrium

An object is in translational equilibrium when the resultant force on it is zero, $\Sigma F = 0$. By the first law it is then at rest or moving at constant velocity — its acceleration is zero. The working method is to resolve every force into perpendicular components (usually horizontal and vertical, or along and perpendicular to a slope) and set the total in each direction to zero. Equilibrium problems are really two simultaneous equations, one per direction.

items: Equilibrium means $\Sigma F = 0$, hence $a = 0$ — NOT necessarily at rest (constant velocity also counts).
Resolve forces into perpendicular directions and set the sum in each to zero.
On a horizontal surface $N = mg$, but on an incline the normal force is $N = mg\cos\theta$ — always take it from the diagram.
A body moving at constant velocity through a fluid (terminal velocity) is in equilibrium: driving force = drag.

sections.question: A 5.0 kg block sits on a rough horizontal floor and is pulled by a horizontal force of 20 N. The coefficient of kinetic friction between block and floor is 0.25. (a) Draw a free-body diagram and find the normal force. (b) Calculate the acceleration of the block. Take $g = 9.81$ N kg⁻¹. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — free-body diagram.** Four forces act on the block: weight $mg$ down, normal force $N$ up, applied force $P = 20$ N to the right, kinetic friction $f$ to the left (opposing the sliding). [M1: correct free-body diagram / forces identified]

**Step 2 — vertical direction (equilibrium).** There is no vertical acceleration, so
$N = mg = 5.0 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \approx 49$ N. [A1: correct normal force]

**Step 3 — friction force.**
$f = \mu N = 0.25 \times 49.05 = 12.3$ N.

**Step 4 — Newton's second law, horizontal direction.**
$\Sigma F = P - f = 20 - 12.3 = 7.7$ N. [M1: resultant force = applied − friction]
$a = \dfrac{\Sigma F}{m} = \dfrac{7.7}{5.0} = 1.5$ m s⁻². [A1]

*The normal force here equals the weight only because the pull is horizontal; if the 20 N were angled upward, $N$ — and therefore the friction — would be smaller.*

sections.content: Newton's second law, momentum and impulse

Momentum is the quantity of motion an object carries: $p = mv$, a vector pointing along the velocity, measured in kg m s⁻¹. Newton actually framed his second law in terms of momentum — the resultant force is the rate of change of momentum, $F = \Delta p / \Delta t$ — and for a constant mass this becomes the familiar $F = ma$. Rearranging gives impulse: $J = F\Delta t = \Delta p$. The impulse of a force is its size multiplied by the time it acts, and it equals the change in momentum produced. On a force–time graph, impulse is the area under the curve. This is why a longer contact time means a smaller force for the same change in momentum — the physics behind airbags, crumple zones and bending your knees when you land.
F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} \qquad p = mv \qquad J = F\Delta t = \Delta p

In impact problems always work with the CHANGE in momentum as a vector. A ball of mass $m$ hitting a wall at speed $v$ and bouncing straight back at speed $v$ does not change its momentum by zero — it changes by $\Delta p = mv - (-mv) = 2mv$, because the direction reverses. Forgetting the sign of the rebound and writing $\Delta p = 0$ is a classic and costly error.
Conservation of linear momentum

When no external resultant force acts on a system, its total momentum stays constant: $\Sigma p_{\text{before}} = \Sigma p_{\text{after}}$. This follows directly from Newton's third law — the internal forces two colliding bodies exert on each other are equal and opposite, so they change the two momenta by equal and opposite amounts, leaving the total unchanged. Because momentum is a vector, you must choose a positive direction and give each velocity the correct sign. Conservation of momentum applies to every collision and every explosion, and it is the single most powerful tool in this topic.

items: Choose a positive direction FIRST, then assign signed velocities.
Total momentum before = total momentum after, in each direction.
In an EXPLOSION the total momentum before is often zero, so the fragments carry equal and opposite momenta.
Momentum is conserved even when kinetic energy is not.

sections.question: A spring-loaded trolley of mass 2.0 kg is held in contact with a stationary trolley of mass 3.0 kg on a frictionless track. When the spring is released, the 2.0 kg trolley moves off to the left at 3.0 m s⁻¹. Calculate the velocity of the 3.0 kg trolley. [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — set up conservation of momentum.** Before release both trolleys are at rest, so the total momentum is zero. Take 'to the right' as positive. [M1: total momentum before = 0]

$\Sigma p_{\text{before}} = \Sigma p_{\text{after}}$
$0 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$

**Step 2 — substitute.** The 2.0 kg trolley moves left, so $v_1 = -3.0$ m s⁻¹:
$0 = (2.0)(-3.0) + (3.0)\,v_2$
$0 = -6.0 + 3.0\,v_2$. [M1]

**Step 3 — solve.**
$v_2 = \dfrac{6.0}{3.0} = +2.0$ m s⁻¹, i.e. 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right. [A1]

*This is an explosion: the two trolleys fly apart carrying equal and opposite momenta (6.0 kg m s⁻¹ each), so the total stays zero. Kinetic energy, however, is created from the spring's stored energy, so it is NOT conserved.*

sections.content: Elastic and inelastic collisions

Momentum is conserved in every collision, but kinetic energy is not. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy is the same before and after — a good model for collisions between hard spheres or between atoms. In an inelastic collision some kinetic energy is transferred into heat, sound and permanent deformation, so the kinetic energy after is less than before. The extreme case is a perfectly inelastic collision, where the objects stick together and move off with a common velocity; this loses the most kinetic energy consistent with conserving momentum. The test is simple: calculate the total kinetic energy before and after — if it is unchanged the collision is elastic, and if it has fallen the collision is inelastic.

items: Elastic: momentum conserved AND kinetic energy conserved.
Inelastic: momentum conserved but kinetic energy NOT conserved (some becomes heat/sound/deformation).
Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together; maximum kinetic energy is lost.
To classify a collision, always compare $\Sigma E_k$ before with $\Sigma E_k$ after.

sections.content: Uniform circular motion

An object moving in a circle at constant speed is still accelerating, because its velocity — a vector — is continually changing direction. This acceleration points toward the centre of the circle and has magnitude $a = v^2/r = \omega^2 r$, where $\omega$ is the angular speed. By Newton's second law a net inward force is therefore required, called the centripetal force: $F = mv^2/r = m\omega^2 r$, always directed toward the centre. It is essential to understand that centripetal force is not a new or extra force — it is the name for the resultant of the ordinary forces (tension, friction, gravity, normal force) resolved toward the centre. There is no outward 'centrifugal' force on the object; the sensation of being flung outward is simply inertia, the body's tendency to continue in a straight line.
a = \frac{v^2}{r} \qquad F = \frac{mv^2}{r} = m\omega^2 r

sections.question: A 900 kg car travels around a flat circular bend of radius 25 m at a constant speed of 12 m s⁻¹. (a) Calculate the centripetal force required. (b) State what provides this force, and find the minimum coefficient of friction needed. Take $g = 9.81$ N kg⁻¹. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — centripetal force.** The net inward force needed is
$F = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} = \dfrac{900 \times 12^2}{25}$. [M1: correct formula and substitution]
$F = \dfrac{900 \times 144}{25} = \dfrac{129600}{25} = 5184 \approx 5.2 \times 10^{3}$ N. [A1]

**Step 2 — source of the force.** On a flat road the only horizontal force available is friction between the tyres and the road, directed toward the centre of the bend. So friction provides the centripetal force. [A1]

**Step 3 — minimum coefficient of friction.** Friction must be at least 5184 N, and the maximum friction is $f = \mu N = \mu mg$:
$\mu \ge \dfrac{F}{mg} = \dfrac{5184}{900 \times 9.81} = 0.59$. [A1]

*If the road is icy and $\mu$ falls below 0.59, friction cannot supply 5184 N, the car cannot turn tightly enough, and it slides outward along a straighter path — not because of any outward force, but because the inward force is now too small.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating a Newton's third-law pair as balanced forces on one body** — the pair acts on TWO different bodies and never cancels; equilibrium is about the several forces acting on ONE body.
**Saying kinetic energy is conserved in every collision** — it is conserved only in elastic collisions; inelastic collisions conserve momentum but lose kinetic energy to heat, sound and deformation.

**Adding a centripetal (or 'centrifugal') force to a free-body diagram** — the centripetal force is the NET inward force already supplied by tension, friction or gravity, not an extra arrow; there is no outward force on the object.
**Getting the friction direction wrong** — friction opposes the relative motion (or its tendency), so it points backward along the surface, never in the direction of motion.
**Ignoring the vector nature of momentum** — assign signs to directions; a ball bouncing back changes momentum by $2mv$, not zero.
**Using $N = mg$ on an incline or in an accelerating lift** — the normal force must be found from the free-body diagram, and equals $mg$ only on a level surface with no vertical acceleration.
**Confusing mass and weight** — mass (kg) is the same everywhere; weight (N) is the force $mg$ and changes with $g$.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is the showcase for a calculation topic. In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific piece of working — a method mark (M) for a correct approach, or an answer mark (A) for a correct result — and, crucially, method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) mean a wrong number early on does not cost you every mark that follows. That protection only exists if your method is written down. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: A 2.0 kg trolley moving at 3.0 m s⁻¹ collides with and sticks to a stationary 1.0 kg trolley on a frictionless track. Calculate the common velocity after the collision, and state whether kinetic energy is conserved. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Common velocity (conservation of momentum).** Take the initial direction of motion as positive. The trolleys stick together, so after the collision they share one velocity $v$.
$\Sigma p_{\text{before}} = \Sigma p_{\text{after}}$
$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = (m_1 + m_2)\,v$
$(2.0)(3.0) + (1.0)(0) = (2.0 + 1.0)\,v$
$6.0 = 3.0\,v$
$v = 2.0$ m s⁻¹, in the original direction of motion.

**Is kinetic energy conserved?** Compare the totals.
$E_{k,\text{before}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2.0)(3.0)^2 = 9.0$ J.
$E_{k,\text{after}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(3.0)(2.0)^2 = 6.0$ J.
The kinetic energy has fallen from 9.0 J to 6.0 J, so **kinetic energy is NOT conserved — the collision is inelastic**. The missing 3.0 J is transferred to heat, sound and deformation as the trolleys couple.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — conservation of momentum set-up.** Awarded for writing total momentum before = total momentum after with the masses and velocities correctly placed, i.e. $(2.0)(3.0) + (1.0)(0) = (2.0+1.0)v$. This is a METHOD mark: it is earned for the correct equation even if the arithmetic later slips.
- **A1 — common velocity.** Awarded for the correct result $v = 2.0$ m s⁻¹ (with direction). Under **error-carried-forward**, a student who set the momentum equation up correctly but mis-added the masses still earns this mark if their velocity follows correctly from THEIR figures.
- **M1 — kinetic energy before vs after.** Awarded for calculating both $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ values so they can be compared — $9.0$ J before and $6.0$ J after. ECF applies: the engine recomputes $E_{k,\text{after}}$ from the student's own value of $v$, so a wrong $v$ carried forward correctly still earns the comparison mark.
- **A1 — 'inelastic' conclusion.** Awarded for stating that kinetic energy is not conserved / the collision is inelastic, consistent with the student's own before-and-after figures.

**'Accept answers that round correctly.'** The engine accepts $v = 2.0$ m s⁻¹ however the intermediate lines are rounded, and accepts kinetic energies quoted to a sensible number of significant figures. It credits the correct method and a correctly-rounded final answer, not a specific number of decimal places.

**Bottom line:** of the 4 marks, 2 are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip and the answer marks are protected by ECF. A student who writes only 'v = 2.0 m/s, inelastic' with no working risks losing 2 marks if a number is wrong; a student who shows the momentum equation and both kinetic energies keeps the method marks regardless.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with ECF. So always show the method: write the conservation-of-momentum equation, substitute with signed velocities, and for a collision always compute the kinetic energy before AND after so your 'elastic or inelastic' verdict is backed by numbers. The method marks and error-carried-forward are what protect your score when the final number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

These ideas underpin the rest of mechanics and beyond. The work–energy topic (A.3) recasts forces acting over distances as energy transfers; gravitational and orbital motion set the gravitational force equal to $mv^2/r$ to find orbital speeds; and in later topics the same momentum bookkeeping reappears for photons, nuclear reactions and rocket propulsion. Master the core habits here — draw the free-body diagram, apply $\Sigma F = ma$ or conservation of momentum, and resolve toward the centre for circular motion — and the rest of physics becomes variations on methods you already own.
